62f3ed6a57b6d50239b523fd.question: To restore part of this artwork, scholars looked at coins depicting a figure sitting in a similar pose and stenciled a goddess sitting atop a pile of weapons. This artwork’s interior contains cow-skull bucrania knobs which are mixed with garlands. Its eastern side contains a panel depicting two goddesses sitting on a swan and a sea dragon, animals that respectively symbolize the air and the ocean. Scholars often debate who exactly is depicted in the so-called “Tellus” panel of this artwork. Though it currently sits in a pavilion designed by (*) Richard Meier, it was once covered with a shell by Vittorio Morpurgo, who helped move this work next to the mausoleum of the ruler it honors. Sections of this work are thought to depict scenes from Book VII of the Aeneid. It originally stood on the Campus Martius and depicts the title goddess next to members of the Roman Senate. For 10 points, what sculpture commissioned by Augustus has a name that translates as “Altar of Peace?”
62f3ed6a57b6d50239b523fd.question_sanitized: To restore part of this artwork, scholars looked at coins depicting a figure sitting in a similar pose and stenciled a goddess sitting atop a pile of weapons. This artwork's interior contains cow-skull bucrania knobs which are mixed with garlands. Its eastern side contains a panel depicting two goddesses sitting on a swan and a sea dragon, animals that respectively symbolize the air and the ocean. Scholars often debate who exactly is depicted in the so-called "Tellus" panel of this artwork. Though it currently sits in a pavilion designed by (*) Richard Meier, it was once covered with a shell by Vittorio Morpurgo, who helped move this work next to the mausoleum of the ruler it honors. Sections of this work are thought to depict scenes from Book VII of the Aeneid. It originally stood on the Campus Martius and depicts the title goddess next to members of the Roman Senate. For 10 points, what sculpture commissioned by Augustus has a name that translates as "Altar of Peace?"
62f3ed6a57b6d50239b523fd.answer_sanitized: Ara Pacis [accept Altar of Peace before "Altar"]

62f3ed6a57b6d50239b52412.question: At the end of a monologue in this play, a character describes “mortal life” as “but a shadow,” and concludes that “[o]ne may be luckier than another when it comes to wealth – but no one is happy, ever.” In another monologue in this play, a character mourns while imagining “the best end that a mortal can enjoy” through her “posthumous eye,” but derides the “hateful weakness to let such tender thoughts erode [her] settled purpose.” That protagonist of this play earlier maintains that she “would rather stand three times in (*) battle with shield and spear than give birth once,” and offers to “make the barren tree blossom” in exchange for Aegeus’s sanctuary. A woman is transformed into a torch by a poisoned golden crown and robe in this play, which ends with the protagonist predicting a “coward's end” for her former lover before escaping in Helios’s dragon-drawn chariot. For 10 points, name this Euripides tragedy whose title character gets revenge on Jason.
62f3ed6a57b6d50239b52412.question_sanitized: At the end of a monologue in this play, a character describes "mortal life" as "but a shadow," and concludes that "[o]ne may be luckier than another when it comes to wealth - but no one is happy, ever." In another monologue in this play, a character mourns while imagining "the best end that a mortal can enjoy" through her "posthumous eye," but derides the "hateful weakness to let such tender thoughts erode [her] settled purpose." That protagonist of this play earlier maintains that she "would rather stand three times in (*) battle with shield and spear than give birth once," and offers to "make the barren tree blossom" in exchange for Aegeus's sanctuary. A woman is transformed into a torch by a poisoned golden crown and robe in this play, which ends with the protagonist predicting a "coward's end" for her former lover before escaping in Helios's dragon-drawn chariot. For 10 points, name this Euripides tragedy whose title character gets revenge on Jason.

62f3ed6a57b6d50239b52461.question: E. B. Harrison suggested that this artwork should be read as depicting chronologically different instances, and that its depiction of 60 riders divided into ten groups is a reference to a historical division into ten tribes. Another part of this artwork shows a seated figure attended by three maidens in what is called the “peplos scene.” Joan Breton Connolly’s controversial interpretation of this artwork, which opposes the traditional Stuart-Everett interpretation, suggests its central subject is the myth of (*) Erechtheus, rather than a procession for athletic games. Approximately 420 feet of this artwork survives and it is mostly housed in the British Museum. The Elgin Marbles include components of, for 10 points, what series of marble relief carvings probably completed under the direction of Phidias, which adorned the side of an Athenian temple?
62f3ed6a57b6d50239b52461.question_sanitized: E. B. Harrison suggested that this artwork should be read as depicting chronologically different instances, and that its depiction of 60 riders divided into ten groups is a reference to a historical division into ten tribes. Another part of this artwork shows a seated figure attended by three maidens in what is called the "peplos scene." Joan Breton Connolly's controversial interpretation of this artwork, which opposes the traditional Stuart-Everett interpretation, suggests its central subject is the myth of (*) Erechtheus, rather than a procession for athletic games. Approximately 420 feet of this artwork survives and it is mostly housed in the British Museum. The Elgin Marbles include components of, for 10 points, what series of marble relief carvings probably completed under the direction of Phidias, which adorned the side of an Athenian temple?
62f3ed6a57b6d50239b52461.answer_sanitized: Parthenon Frieze [prompt on Parthenon or Elgin Marbles]

62f3ed6a57b6d50239b5248b.question: Art historian Kenneth Lapatin suggested that sculptures of this figure are forgeries intended for 20th-century art markets in a book titled for this figure’s “mysteries.” A votary depiction of this figure was successfully re-assembled and held together with paraffin after a “hat” with a cat on top of it was identified. This example of the potnia theron archetype is given a runner with inkle-loom woven strips in Judy Chicago’s Dinner Party, in which the plate set aside for her has a color scheme that evokes chryselephantine. The most iconic glazed earthenware sculptures of this figure depict her with (*) bared breasts, show her wearing a corset-like garment around her narrow waist, and were unearthed by Arthur Evans at Knossos. For 10 points, name this goddess depicted in many Minoan figurines, who is named for holding two certain animals in her hands.
62f3ed6a57b6d50239b5248b.question_sanitized: Art historian Kenneth Lapatin suggested that sculptures of this figure are forgeries intended for 20th-century art markets in a book titled for this figure's "mysteries." A votary depiction of this figure was successfully re-assembled and held together with paraffin after a "hat" with a cat on top of it was identified. This example of the potnia theron archetype is given a runner with inkle-loom woven strips in Judy Chicago's Dinner Party, in which the plate set aside for her has a color scheme that evokes chryselephantine. The most iconic glazed earthenware sculptures of this figure depict her with (*) bared breasts, show her wearing a corset-like garment around her narrow waist, and were unearthed by Arthur Evans at Knossos. For 10 points, name this goddess depicted in many Minoan figurines, who is named for holding two certain animals in her hands.
62f3ed6a57b6d50239b5248b.answer: Minoan snake goddess [accept snake after “goddess”; accept Mysteries of the Snake Goddess; prompt on goddess or Minoan goddess]
62f3ed6a57b6d50239b5248b.answer_sanitized: Minoan snake goddess [accept snake after "goddess"; accept Mysteries of the Snake Goddess; prompt on goddess or Minoan goddess]

62f3ed6a57b6d50239b5249c.question: A character in this play roasts the slave Lurcio for being drunk off of his master’s wine and instructs another character to dress as a sailor to deliver a fake message to his lover. Another character in this play pretends to be her own identical twin by passing through a hidden hole in the wall between two onstage houses in an attempt to silence a slave who had seen her secretly kissing her lover through a rooftop skylight. In this play, the courtesan Arcoteleutium is paid to pretend to love a character who claims to have (*) killed seven thousand men “at one fell blow” with his sidekick Artotrogus. Philocomesium escapes Ephesus with her lover Pleusicles and the cunning slave Palaestrio (“pah-LYE-stree-oh”) after being kidnapped by the title character of this play, who is ultimately lured into Periplectomenus’s house and beaten for his arrogance. For 10 points, name this Plautus comedy in which Pyrgopolices (“peer-goh-POH-lee-kays”) is the title boasting officer.
62f3ed6a57b6d50239b5249c.question_sanitized: A character in this play roasts the slave Lurcio for being drunk off of his master's wine and instructs another character to dress as a sailor to deliver a fake message to his lover. Another character in this play pretends to be her own identical twin by passing through a hidden hole in the wall between two onstage houses in an attempt to silence a slave who had seen her secretly kissing her lover through a rooftop skylight. In this play, the courtesan Arcoteleutium is paid to pretend to love a character who claims to have (*) killed seven thousand men "at one fell blow" with his sidekick Artotrogus. Philocomesium escapes Ephesus with her lover Pleusicles and the cunning slave Palaestrio ("pah-LYE-stree-oh") after being kidnapped by the title character of this play, who is ultimately lured into Periplectomenus's house and beaten for his arrogance. For 10 points, name this Plautus comedy in which Pyrgopolices ("peer-goh-POH-lee-kays") is the title boasting officer.
62f3ed6a57b6d50239b5249c.answer: Miles Gloriosus [or The Braggart Soldier or The Swaggart Soldier or The Vainglorious Soldier]
62f3ed6a57b6d50239b5249c.answer_sanitized: Miles Gloriosus [or The Braggart Soldier or The Swaggart Soldier or The Vainglorious Soldier]
